Widespread use of Network Security Groups (957 NSGs, heavily concentrated in eastus): tenant practice is to apply NSGs broadly for segmentation and access control, so plan to implement dedicated NSGs per tier (management, application, data) and attach them to the corresponding subnets to follow the established security posture.

All existing Azure Load Balancers are Standard SKU (100%): the tenant standardizes on Standard/Regional LBs which support internal load balancing and predictable behavior, so deploy the internal Azure Load Balancer as Standard SKU and name backend pools/frontend configs consistently with existing pool naming conventions.

Public IP addresses are overwhelmingly Standard SKU (95.9%): the tenant preference is for Standard public IPs for consistent security and performance, therefore if the jumpbox requires a public IP use a Standard SKU or avoid a public IP entirely by using Bastion or private access methods.

Bastion hosts are already deployed (7 instances, 71% using Developer SKU): the tenant uses Bastion as a management option, so consider using an existing Bastion host for secure jumpbox access instead of exposing a VM with a public IP to reduce attack surface.

Virtual networks show subnet naming conventions including default, appSubnet and endpointSubnet: this indicates tier-aligned subnet design, so create a management/managementSubnet for the jumpbox and place application tiers in appSubnet/endpointSubnet while applying NSGs per subnet to match conventions.

Private DNS zones and many private endpoints are present (74 private DNS zones, 56 private endpoints): the tenant favors private name resolution and private connectivity, so register the internal load balancer’s private IP in the private DNS zone and use private DNS records for inter-tier communication.

Virtual machines almost always have managed identities (98.8% SystemAssigned, UserAssigned): the tenant leverages managed identities for secure resource access, so enable a managed identity on the jumpbox VM to retrieve secrets (e.g., SSH keys) from Key Vault without embedding credentials.

NAT Gateways and Standard SKUs are in use (19 NAT Gateways, Standard SKU): the tenant commonly controls outbound egress via NAT Gateway, so place the management subnet or jumpbox behind a NAT Gateway to centralize and control outbound traffic rather than giving the jumpbox direct internet connectivity.

Key Vaults use purge protection and long soft-delete retention (enablepurgeprotection True, softDelete 90 days) but have mixed network ACL defaults: the tenant protects secrets by default but varies network restrictions, so store jumpbox credentials in Key Vault with purge protection and explicitly restrict Key Vault network ACLs to the management subnet or IP ranges used for admin access.
